text.content: MINI PRD: P100 — Filter-Press Feed Pump Curve Drift
Batch: 10 | Priority: P100 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals pain point of filter-press feed pump operating off its curve as filter cake builds (causing pressure rise, flow drop, and inefficient cycle times) and show how FluidFlow's pump curve analysis models this dynamic resistance change.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Filter presses start each cycle with low resistance (clean filter cloths), then build pressure as cake accumulates. Pumps sized for mid-cycle conditions either stall at end-of-cycle or waste energy at start. FluidFlow's pump curve analysis models this resistance change, showing duty point drift across the cycle.
**Pain point mapping**: Fixed pump sizing → duty point drift as filter resistance increases → stall (flow drop) or energy waste → inefficient cycle times → higher operating cost.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid or Slurry Module pump curve modeling, system curve overlay, duty point tracking across resistance range. Show how changing filter K-factor (0.5 to 5.0 as cake builds) shifts duty point along pump curve.

**Proof element**: Model filter-press feed pump at three cycle stages: clean (K=0.5), mid-cycle (K=2.0), end-cycle (K=5.0). Show duty point shift from 120 m³/h @ 8 bar to 80 m³/h @ 12 bar. Demonstrate pump selection that stays within curve limits across full range.
SIDEWAYS: P98 Thickener Overflow NPSH Risk (Mining), P105 Pump Selection vs System Head Curve (Utilities), P119 Parallel Pump Staging (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Module**: Liquid or Slurry (depending on slurry concentration)
**Capabilities**: Pump curve modeling, system curve overlay, duty point tracking, K-factor resistance modeling
MINI PRD: P101 — Dust Suppression Grids
Batch: 10 | Priority: P101 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals pain point of dust suppression spray grid pressure imbalance (causing uneven coverage—some nozzles over-spray, others under-deliver—failing to meet environmental compliance) and show how FluidFlow's multi-branch network analysis ensures uniform nozzle pressure.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Dust suppression spray grids require uniform pressure at each nozzle to achieve target droplet size and coverage. Under-sized headers create pressure gradients—nozzles near the feed over-spray, distant ones under-deliver—leaving dust hotspots. FluidFlow's multi-branch analysis ensures even pressure distribution.
**Pain point mapping**: Under-sized header → pressure gradient across grid → uneven nozzle flow → inadequate coverage → dust compliance failure → regulatory fines.
**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module multi-branch network solver, pressure distribution mapping, nozzle (orifice) modeling using ISO 5167, automatic velocity/pressure alerts.

**Proof element**: Model 12-nozzle spray grid with 4" header. Show pressure drop from first nozzle (6.5 bar) to last nozzle (4.2 bar). Demonstrate how increasing header to 6" reduces gradient to 6.5 bar → 6.1 bar, achieving uniform spray.
SIDEWAYS: P97 Ring Main Diameter Selection (Mining), P99 Series/Parallel Pipelines (Mining), P108 Booster Stations Across Elevation (Utilities)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Multi-branch network solver, pressure distribution mapping, orifice modeling (ISO 5167), automatic velocity/pressure alerts (part of 118 design alerts)
MINI PRD: P102 — Cooling-Water Multi-Pump Combinations
Batch: 10 | Priority: P102 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals pain point of cooling-water pump staging (running 2-of-3 or 3-of-4 pumps to match seasonal demand, but causing flow/pressure instability when switching combinations) and show how FluidFlow's parallel pump analysis predicts system behavior for each staging scenario.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Smelters and concentrators run cooling-water pumps in parallel, staging them (1, 2, or 3 pumps online) to match seasonal heat load. Switching pump combinations shifts system flow and pressure unpredictably, risking equipment trips. FluidFlow's parallel pump analysis predicts each staging scenario, ensuring stable operation.
**Pain point mapping**: Parallel pump staging → unpredictable flow/pressure shift → equipment trips (cooling water shortage) → unplanned downtime → production loss.
**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module parallel pump modeling, combined pump curve generation, system curve overlay, duty point tracking for each staging combination (1-pump, 2-pump, 3-pump).

**Proof element**: Model 3 parallel cooling-water pumps (each 500 m³/h @ 5 bar). Show combined system duty points: 1-pump = 450 m³/h @ 4.8 bar, 2-pump = 920 m³/h @ 5.1 bar, 3-pump = 1,350 m³/h @ 5.3 bar. Demonstrate how to verify minimum flow (1-pump) and maximum pressure (3-pump) stay within equipment limits.
SIDEWAYS: P97 Ring Main Diameter Selection (Mining), P100 Filter-Press Feed Pump Curve Drift (Mining), P107 Parallel Pump Staging (Utilities), P119 Parallel Pump Staging (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Parallel pump modeling, combined pump curve generation, system curve overlay, duty point tracking
MINI PRD: P103 — Two-Phase Tank Vents
Batch: 10 | Priority: P103 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals pain point of two-phase venting from flash tanks (causing vent line under-sizing, back-pressure buildup, and tank over-pressurization) and show how FluidFlow's Two-Phase Module models vapor/liquid mixtures to size vent lines correctly.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Flash tanks in mining hydrometallurgy (autoclave discharge, pressure letdown) vent two-phase mixtures—vapor with entrained liquid. Under-sized vent lines create back-pressure, forcing the tank to over-pressurize or liquid to carry over. FluidFlow's Two-Phase Module models this mixture, sizing vents to avoid both.
**Pain point mapping**: Under-sized two-phase vent → back-pressure buildup → tank over-pressurization or liquid carryover → safety trips → downtime.

**FluidFlow capability**: Two-Phase Module flash calculation, vapor quality tracking, flow regime mapping, pressure drop correlation (Friedel, Lockhart-Martinelli, etc.), automatic choked flow detection.

**Proof element**: Model autoclave flash tank vent (10 bar → atmospheric, 180°C water, 5% vapor quality). Show 4" vent creates back-pressure (2.1 bar residual), while 6" vent achieves target (1.2 bar). Demonstrate flow regime map (annular flow) and choked flow warning if under-sized.
SIDEWAYS: P104 Fire-Water Tie-Ins (Mining), P111 Two-Phase PRV Venting (Utilities), P116 Two-Phase Outlet Checks (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.

**Capabilities**: Flash calculation, vapor quality tracking, flow regime mapping, pressure drop correlation (8 options: Friedel, Lockhart-Martinelli, Beggs & Brill, etc.), automatic choked flow detection
**Correlations**: Friedel, Lockhart-Martinelli, Whalley Criteria (selects appropriate correlation)
**Databases**: 1,283 fluids (water with phase change via EOS: Peng-Robinson, Lee-Kesler, BWRHS)
Batch: 10 | Priority: P104 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals pain point of fire-water system tie-ins to existing mains (causing pressure drop that starves existing users when new branch activates) and show how FluidFlow's multi-branch network analysis verifies adequate pressure at all demand points simultaneously.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Adding fire-water tie-ins to existing mains risks starving existing hydrants when the new branch demands flow. Mine sites must prove all fire-water users receive minimum code pressure (7 bar typical) simultaneously. FluidFlow's multi-branch analysis models all demand points at once, verifying compliance.
**Pain point mapping**: New fire-water tie-in → pressure drop in main → existing hydrants below code minimum → failed inspection → delayed project approval.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module multi-branch network solver, simultaneous demand modeling (all hydrants flowing at once), pressure distribution mapping, automatic pressure alert (one of 118 design alerts).

**Proof element**: Model existing fire-water main with 4 hydrants (each 50 L/s @ 7 bar min). Add new tie-in (2 hydrants, 100 L/s total). Show pressure at existing hydrant #4 drops to 6.1 bar (below code). Demonstrate correction: upsize main from 8" to 10", achieving 7.3 bar minimum at all points.
SIDEWAYS: P97 Ring Main Diameter Selection (Mining), P101 Dust Suppression Grids (Mining), P108 Booster Stations Across Elevation (Utilities)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Multi-branch network solver, simultaneous demand modeling, pressure distribution mapping, automatic pressure alert (part of 118 design alerts)
MINI PRD: P105 — Pump Selection vs System Head Curve
Batch: 11 | Priority: P105 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of pump/system curve mismatch (causing pumps to operate off-curve—either dead-heading or running out—wasting energy or causing cavitation) and show how FluidFlow's system curve overlay ensures pumps operate at peak efficiency.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Pumps perform efficiently only when their duty point matches the system curve. Utilities engineers who size pumps without modeling the full system risk dead-heading (high pressure, low flow, wasted energy) or running out (low pressure, high flow, cavitation). FluidFlow overlays system curve on pump curve, ensuring efficient duty point selection.
**Pain point mapping**: Pump sized without system curve → duty point mismatch → energy waste (dead-heading) or cavitation (running out) → high operating cost or pump failure.
**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module system curve generation, pump curve overlay, duty point identification, efficiency tracking, NPSH verification, automatic cavitation/efficiency alerts.

**Proof element**: Model water distribution booster with system curve (static head 45m, friction 1.2 bar/km). Show candidate pump A (duty point at 82% efficiency) vs pump B (duty point at 68% efficiency). Calculate annual energy cost difference. Show NPSH margin at duty point.
SIDEWAYS: P97 Ring Main Diameter Selection (Mining), P100 Filter-Press Feed Pump Curve Drift (Mining), P107 Parallel Pump Staging (Utilities)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: System curve generation, pump curve overlay, duty point identification, efficiency tracking, NPSH calculation, automatic cavitation/efficiency alerts (part of 118 design alerts)
MINI PRD: P106 — Cooling-Water Loop Diameter Selection
Batch: 11 | Priority: P106 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of cooling-water loop over-sizing (causing excessive capital cost) or under-sizing (causing pumps to over-work, wasting energy) and show how FluidFlow's economic velocity calculation balances capex vs opex.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Cooling-water loops trade capital cost (larger pipe = higher cost) against operating cost (smaller pipe = higher pumping energy). Over-sizing wastes capex, under-sizing wastes opex. FluidFlow's economic velocity calculation (Generaux equation) finds the diameter where total lifecycle cost is minimized.
**Pain point mapping**: Incorrect diameter → either excessive capex (over-sized) or excessive opex (under-sized, high pumping cost) → suboptimal lifecycle cost.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), pump energy calculation, lifecycle cost comparison (manual or via Scripting Module for automated analysis).

**Proof element**: Model 500m cooling-water loop, 200 m³/h, 10-year lifecycle. Compare 8", 10", 12" pipe: show capex (pipe cost), opex (pump energy kWh/year), and total. Demonstrate economic velocity selects 10" as optimal (lowest total cost). Cite Generaux equation.
SIDEWAYS: P97 Ring Main Diameter Selection (Mining), P112 Pipe Diameter Optimization (Utilities), P113 Cooling-Water Loop Pipe Diameter (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), pump energy calculation, lifecycle cost comparison (via Scripting Module for automation)
MINI PRD: P107 — Parallel Pump Staging
Batch: 11 | Priority: P107 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of parallel pump staging (running 1, 2, or 3 pumps to match diurnal demand, but causing flow instability or dead-heading when switching) and show how FluidFlow's parallel pump analysis predicts system behavior for each staging scenario.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Water utilities run pumps in parallel, staging them (1-pump at night, 2-pump at midday, 3-pump at peak) to match demand. Switching staging shifts duty point unpredictably—1-pump may dead-head, 3-pump may exceed pipe velocity limits. FluidFlow's parallel pump analysis models each staging scenario, ensuring stable operation across all combinations.
**Pain point mapping**: Parallel pump staging → unpredictable duty point shift → dead-heading (energy waste) or velocity exceedance (erosion risk) → inefficiency or pipe damage.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module parallel pump modeling, combined pump curve generation, system curve overlay, duty point tracking for each staging scenario, automatic velocity/pressure alerts.

**Proof element**: Model 3 parallel water distribution pumps (each 150 m³/h @ 6 bar). Show combined system duty points: 1-pump = 130 m³/h @ 5.8 bar (near shut-off, 52% efficiency), 2-pump = 280 m³/h @ 6.1 bar (78% efficiency), 3-pump = 420 m³/h @ 6.3 bar (81% efficiency, but 3.2 m/s velocity—above 3.0 m/s erosion limit). Demonstrate how to select pumps that stay within efficiency/velocity limits across all staging scenarios.
SIDEWAYS: P102 Cooling-Water Multi-Pump Combinations (Mining), P105 Pump Selection vs System Head Curve (Utilities), P119 Parallel Pump Staging (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Parallel pump modeling, combined pump curve generation, system curve overlay, duty point tracking, automatic velocity/pressure alerts (part of 118 design alerts)
MINI PRD: P108 — Booster Stations Across Elevation
Batch: 11 | Priority: P108 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of booster station placement in hilly terrain (causing either excessive pressure at low points—risking pipe burst—or insufficient pressure at high points—failing to meet minimum service pressure) and show how FluidFlow's elevation modeling optimizes booster location and pump sizing.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Water distribution across elevation changes risks over-pressurizing low points (pipe burst) or under-pressurizing high points (service failure). Utilities must place booster stations to keep all points within pressure limits (typically 2-10 bar). FluidFlow's elevation modeling shows pressure at every node, guiding booster placement.
**Pain point mapping**: Incorrect booster placement → either over-pressure at low points (pipe burst risk) or under-pressure at high points (service failure) → asset damage or customer complaints.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module elevation (static head) modeling, pressure distribution mapping across network, booster pump placement optimization, automatic pressure alerts (one of 118 design alerts).

**Proof element**: Model water distribution from reservoir (elevation 100m) to hilltop consumers (elevation 180m), distance 3 km. Show single pump at reservoir creates 6.2 bar at midpoint (elevation 110m, exceeds 6 bar limit) but only 1.8 bar at hilltop (below 2 bar minimum). Demonstrate solution: two booster stations (at 100m and 140m) achieve 3-5 bar across entire network.
SIDEWAYS: P101 Dust Suppression Grids (Mining), P104 Fire-Water Tie-Ins (Mining), P105 Pump Selection vs System Head Curve (Utilities)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Elevation (static head) modeling, pressure distribution mapping, booster pump placement optimization, automatic pressure alerts (part of 118 design alerts)
**Databases**: 1,283 fluids (water), 14 pipe types (steel, ductile iron, PVC)
MINI PRD: P109 — District Heating Valve Dp
Batch: 11 | Priority: P109 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of district heating control valve Δp selection (causing either poor control authority—valve ineffective—or excessive pressure drop—wasted pump energy) and show how FluidFlow's control valve analysis optimizes Δp for both controllability and efficiency.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): District heating control valves balance two competing goals: high Δp (good control authority, but wastes pump energy) vs low Δp (energy-efficient, but poor control). Incorrect Δp causes either hunting (valve can't stabilize flow) or energy waste. FluidFlow's control valve analysis calculates valve authority and pump energy, optimizing Δp for both.

**Pain point mapping**: Incorrect valve Δp → either poor control authority (hunting, unstable temperature) or excessive pressure drop (wasted pump energy) → customer complaints or high operating cost.
**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module control valve auto-sizing, valve authority calculation, Cv curve modeling, pump energy tracking, system curve overlay to show valve Δp vs pipe Δp.

**Proof element**: Model district heating substation with 50 kW load, 80/60°C supply/return, 2.4 m³/h flow. Compare valve Δp: 0.5 bar (authority 0.28, poor control), 1.0 bar (authority 0.48, adequate control), 2.0 bar (authority 0.67, excellent control but +15% pump energy). Demonstrate optimal Δp = 1.0 bar (authority >0.4, minimizes energy waste).
SIDEWAYS: P110 Cooling Tower Make-up NPSH Margin (Utilities), P114 Chilled-Water Valve Cv and Authority (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Control valve auto-sizing, valve authority calculation, Cv curve modeling, pump energy tracking, system curve overlay
MINI PRD: P110 — Cooling Tower Make-up NPSH Margin
Batch: 11 | Priority: P110 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of cooling tower make-up pump cavitation (caused by low basin level—insufficient NPSHa—forcing emergency trips and overheating) and show how FluidFlow's NPSH analysis ensures adequate margin under low-level conditions.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Cooling tower make-up pumps operate from shallow basins (2-4 meters static head). When basin level drops during high evaporation, NPSHa falls below NPSHr, causing cavitation and emergency trips. FluidFlow's NPSH analysis models worst-case low level, ensuring pumps remain cavitation-free.
**Pain point mapping**: Low basin level → insufficient NPSHa → cavitation → pump trip → cooling water shortage → equipment overheating → unplanned shutdown.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module NPSH calculation (NPSHa vs NPSHr), pump database with NPSHr curves, automatic cavitation alert (one of 118 design alerts), scenario analysis for low-level conditions.

**Proof element**: Model cooling tower make-up pump at two basin levels: normal (3.5m static head, NPSHa = 3.2m, NPSHr = 2.8m, margin = 0.4m) vs low (2.0m static head, NPSHa = 1.5m, NPSHr = 2.8m, CAVITATION). Demonstrate correction: select low-NPSHr pump (NPSHr = 1.2m) or increase basin depth to 3.0m minimum.
SIDEWAYS: P98 Thickener Overflow NPSH Risk (Mining), P109 District Heating Valve Dp (Utilities), P118 PW/CIP NPSH Margin (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: NPSH calculation (NPSHa vs NPSHr), pump database with NPSHr curves, automatic cavitation alert (part of 118 design alerts), scenario analysis (via Scripting Module or manual runs)
MINI PRD: P111 — Two-Phase PRV Venting (Steady-State)
Batch: 11 | Priority: P111 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of two-phase pressure relief valve (PRV) discharge line under-sizing (causing back-pressure that prevents PRV from opening fully, risking equipment over-pressurization) and show how FluidFlow's Two-Phase Module sizes discharge lines to avoid excessive back-pressure.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): PRVs discharging steam or flashing liquids produce two-phase mixtures. Under-sized discharge lines create back-pressure that prevents PRVs from opening fully, risking equipment over-pressurization. FluidFlow's Two-Phase Module models this mixture, sizing discharge lines to keep back-pressure below PRV limits (typically <10% set pressure for steam, <25% for liquids).
**Pain point mapping**: Under-sized PRV discharge line → excessive back-pressure → PRV can't open fully → equipment over-pressurization → safety risk or asset damage.

**FluidFlow capability**: Two-Phase Module flash calculation, vapor quality tracking, flow regime mapping, pressure drop correlation (Friedel, Lockhart-Martinelli, etc.), relief valve modeling (API RP 520 or ISO 4126-1), automatic back-pressure warning.

**Proof element**: Model steam PRV discharge (set pressure 10 bar, 5% vapor quality at inlet, discharge to atmospheric). Show 3" discharge line creates 1.8 bar back-pressure (18% of set pressure, exceeds 10% limit for steam PRV). Demonstrate 4" discharge line reduces back-pressure to 0.7 bar (7%, acceptable). Show flow regime map (annular flow) for both cases.
SIDEWAYS: P103 Two-Phase Tank Vents (Mining), P116 Two-Phase Outlet Checks (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.

**Capabilities**: Flash calculation, vapor quality tracking, flow regime mapping, pressure drop correlation (8 options: Friedel, Lockhart-Martinelli, Beggs & Brill, etc.), relief valve modeling (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1), automatic back-pressure warning
**Correlations**: Friedel, Lockhart-Martinelli, Whalley Criteria (selects appropriate correlation)
**Standards**: API RP 520 (relief valve sizing), ISO 4126-1 (relief valve sizing)
**Databases**: 1,283 fluids (water, steam with phase change via EOS: Peng-Robinson, Lee-Kesler, BWRHS), relief valve database (multiple vendors)
MINI PRD: P112 — Pipe Diameter Optimization
Batch: 11 | Priority: P112 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the utilities pain point of water distribution pipe diameter selection (balancing capital cost vs pumping energy—over-sizing wastes capex, under-sizing wastes opex) and show how FluidFlow's economic velocity calculation optimizes lifecycle cost.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Water distribution pipe diameter trades capital cost (larger pipe = higher cost) against operating cost (smaller pipe = higher pumping energy). Over-sizing wastes capex, under-sizing wastes opex. FluidFlow's economic velocity calculation (Generaux equation) finds the diameter where total lifecycle cost is minimized.
**Pain point mapping**: Incorrect diameter → either excessive capex (over-sized) or excessive opex (under-sized, high pumping cost) → suboptimal lifecycle cost.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), pump energy calculation, lifecycle cost comparison (manual or via Scripting Module for automated analysis).

**Proof element**: Model 2 km water distribution main, 300 m³/h, 20-year lifecycle. Compare 10", 12", 14" pipe: show capex (pipe cost), opex (pump energy kWh/year), and total. Demonstrate economic velocity selects 12" as optimal (lowest total cost). Cite Generaux equation. Include sensitivity analysis: how optimal diameter changes with energy cost ($/kWh).
SIDEWAYS: P99 Series/Parallel Pipelines (Mining), P106 Cooling-Water Loop Diameter Selection (Utilities), P113 Cooling-Water Loop Pipe Diameter (Chemical), P120 Pipe-Size "One-Up" Study (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), pump energy calculation, lifecycle cost comparison (via Scripting Module for automation)
**Databases**: 1,283 fluids (water), 14 pipe types (steel, ductile iron, PVC, HDPE)
MINI PRD: P113 — Cooling-Water Loop Pipe Diameter
Batch: 12 | Priority: P113 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the chemical processing pain point of cooling-water loop diameter selection (balancing capital cost vs pumping energy—over-sizing wastes capex, under-sizing wastes opex) and show how FluidFlow's economic velocity calculation optimizes lifecycle cost.

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Cooling-water loops trade capital cost (larger pipe = higher cost) against operating cost (smaller pipe = higher pumping energy). Over-sizing wastes capex, under-sizing wastes opex. FluidFlow's economic velocity calculation (Generaux equation) finds the diameter where total lifecycle cost is minimized.
**Pain point mapping**: Incorrect diameter → either excessive capex (over-sized) or excessive opex (under-sized, high pumping cost) → suboptimal lifecycle cost.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), pump energy calculation, lifecycle cost comparison (manual or via Scripting Module for automated analysis).

**Proof element**: Model 800m cooling-water loop for reactor cooling, 400 m³/h, 15-year lifecycle. Compare 10", 12", 14" pipe: show capex (pipe cost, stainless for chemical compatibility), opex (pump energy kWh/year), and total. Demonstrate economic velocity selects 12" as optimal (lowest total cost). Cite Generaux equation.
SIDEWAYS: P106 Cooling-Water Loop Diameter Selection (Utilities), P112 Pipe Diameter Optimization (Utilities), P120 Pipe-Size "One-Up" Study (Chemical)
BLOG: Link to relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at creation time)
NONE — This is an industry solution article. No selling message required.
**Capabilities**: Pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), pump energy calculation, lifecycle cost comparison (via Scripting Module for automation)
**Databases**: 1,283 fluids (water), 14 pipe types (stainless steel for chemical compatibility)
